name: Agfa Optima
description: Inspired by the Optima 200 film stock, this recipe was created with versatility in mind. It offers moderate saturation and contrast, paired with a subtle analog-like fade. Since it leans slightly high-key, you may want to underexpose slightly when shooting in harsh sunlight to preserve highlight detail. Enabling Active D-Lighting can help recover shadows, though it will slightly reduce overall contrast.

name: Agfa RSX II
description: Inspired by Agfa’s professional line of slide film, this recipe was designed to be well-balanced for everyday use, with a slightly cooler yet neutral color scheme. It features reduced exposure latitude, snappier contrast and increased sharpness to better mimic the character of slide film. In my field tests, it has performed especially well when capturing architectural details and industrial scenes.

name: Agfa XPS 160
description: Inspired by the look of vintage photos shot on Agfacolor Portrait XPS 160, this recipe leans into a softer, cooler palette with a character reminiscent of old color negative film. While this recipe can work for portraits, I don't think I'll choose it over something like the Rich Tone Portrait profile, unless I'm shooting during cold, rainy days where the cooler tones feel more fitting.

name: Agfachrome Expired
description: The full name for this recipe should be “Expired Agfachrome Slide” as it’s modeled after photos shot on expired color reversal films like Agfachrome 100 RS, RSX II, and CT100. Rather than letting magenta tones overpower the entire image (as often happens with poorly stored slide film), I aimed for a more balanced and warmer color scheme, reminiscent of images taken at sunrise or sunset.

description: Agfacolor S is based on a draft recipe that was supposed to be released alongside the other entries in the Agfa Legacy collection over a year ago. Inspired by Agfachrome CTx 100 (you can find a selection of film scans here), it was tested but never released - for reasons I can no longer remember. Revisiting it now, I reworked the recipe to fit within the Signature series while retaining some elements of its original character.

name: Documentary Chrome
description: This is the recipe that required the most work so far. It is based on a Lightroom preset I created some time ago and have been using regularly in my own photography. The goal is a documentary look with rich contrast, deep shadows, natural but present colors, and realistic skin tones, designed for everyday life photography. The recipe is tuned to be used with D Lighting set to Normal as a baseline. For more contrast and deeper shadows, you can switch to Low. In very contrasty or low light situations, High will reduce contrast and give a brighter, slightly overexposed film like look. As always, this recipe is free to use. However, for those who would like to support my work, you can do so through a donation via my Ko fi link https://ko-fi.com/filmstill__ (or the link in bio). It is always very appreciated, as creating and refining these recipes takes a lot of time and effort. Thank you to everyone who supports and uses my work.
author: Denis Zeqiri (filmstill__)
activeDLighting: Use Normal for baseline, Low for stronger contrast and deeper shadows, High to soften contrast in high-contrast or low-light scenes.

name: Juniper
description: Juniper came from a need for a cooler companion to Hawthorn. It keeps the same mood and underexposed look, but tones down the autumnal warmth. Hawthorn leans warmer and more seasonal: greys drift toward brown, highlights pick up a subtle amber cast, and greens skew olive. Juniper shifts the palette toward colder, cleaner tones: neutrals sit closer to their natural look, shadows feel more blue-green, and greens lean more pine than olive.

name: LUX B&W HC
description: The high-contrast monochrome look needs little introduction. This is a no-nonsense recipe built on Nikon’s Deep Tone Monochrome profile, refined with a custom tone curve that amplifies the contrast. One key advantage is that it works seamlessly in the Zf’s native B&W switch/mode, something I consider a big advantage, even if I don’t shoot black and white all that often. I usually turn off Active D-Lighting when using this recipe. Disabling it helps preserve deep shadows and maximizes the high-contrast look.

name: LUX Brass
description: This recipe introduces a distinct warmth to mid-tones and highlights, while shadows take on a noticeable magenta cast. It gives images a bit of character without feeling overly stylized, leaning toward a somewhat vintage digital aesthetic rather than a film emulation. If you want to lean further into the strenghts of the look, try shifting the white balance slightly warmer (something like A1.50, M0.25 seems to work well in most scenarios).

name: LUX Chrome
description: The Chrome look aims for subtlety, with slightly reduced saturation, gentle contrast, and lifted shadows. It preserves a natural look, making it the most versatile recipe in this set. It works well in scenes where color isn’t meant to tell the story, and I often use it when trying to capture a sense of calm or stillness. That said, green tones (especially in foliage) can sometimes skew too warm, so a more neutral WB may help maintain balance if you’re shooting in mixed or natural light.

name: LUX Eternal
description: This is the boldest recipe in the set, pushing saturation and contrast significantly to produce deep blacks, dense reds, and vivid greens. The ultramarine-magenta cast in the shadows often carries the weight of the look, giving the images a strong presence. The effect becomes less pronounced when shooting slightly overexposed or with Active D-Lighting enabled.
